title: US Adopts Iranian Smuggling Tactics to Keep Gulf Oil Flowing
subhead: The US military has been running secret ship-to-ship transfers to bypass Hormuz, while Ukraine hits Moscow's largest refinery and G7 weighs renewed Russian oil sanctions.

sections.heading: US Military Mimics Iranian Oil Smuggling to Beat Hormuz Blockade
sections.body:
The United States military has overseen scores of secretive ship-to-ship oil transfers to keep Gulf energy exports flowing during the Iran conflict, using aerial and water drones as well as helicopters in an operation to guide convoys to awaiting tankers, Reuters reports. The operation, which had not been previously reported, essentially mirrors the smuggling tactics Iran developed to evade sanctions.

According to Reuters, the US used helicopters and unmanned systems to facilitate transfers that bypassed the Hormuz blockade. The report highlights how the standoff disrupted global energy logistics and forced Washington to adopt methods it had long condemned when Tehran used them.

This represents a significant operational shift for the US Navy, which historically protected commercial shipping lanes rather than facilitating covert transfers. The tactic acknowledges a hard reality: when 95% of crude tanker transits through Hormuz were blocked during the 107-day standoff, conventional naval escort operations couldn't move enough volume.
sections.whyItMatters: The US military adopting Iranian-style smuggling operations to move oil represents a pragmatic but symbolically charged adaptation. It shows how chokepoint warfare forces even dominant naval powers into asymmetric responses, and creates a precedent that Tehran may cite when justifying its own shadow fleet operations going forward.

sections.heading: Middle East Oil and Gas Output Will Take Months to Fully Recover
sections.body:
A framework agreement between the US and Iran to end their war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz sent oil prices tumbling as traders anticipated the return of flows, but analysts warn that full recovery will take months, according to multiple news reports. Infrastructure damage, mine clearance, and insurance market normalization all create lag times.

Brent crude fell sharply on news of the deal, with July WTI closing down 4.87% and gasoline futures also dropping, according to Yahoo Finance. Markets are repricing the geopolitical risk premium, though supply constraints and infrastructure damage persist.

Chinese oil refiners slashed output last month to the weakest level in nearly four years after crude imports plunged to an eight-year low due to the near-halt of Hormuz shipments, Bloomberg reports. The import collapse underscores how dependent China remains on Gulf crude despite diversification efforts.
sections.whyItMatters: The disconnect between diplomatic breakthroughs and physical market recovery is critical. Tankers can return to Hormuz, but cleared lanes, functioning terminals, and normalized insurance rates don't materialize overnight. Energy importers face a transition period where diplomatic calm hasn't yet translated into supply security.

sections.heading: G7 Focuses on Russia Oil Sanctions as Trump Signals Potential Return
sections.body:
US President Donald Trump said Tuesday that Washington could reinstate sanctions on Russian oil as part of a renewed pressure campaign against the Kremlin by G7 leading economies, Politico Europe reports. The US had previously waived certain sanctions, but the G7 summit in France has refocused on Ukraine as the Iran conflict de-escalates.

Trump told reporters that the forthcoming Iran peace deal would be made public 'in a couple of days' and would be sent to Congress for review. He also suggested Syria could be better positioned to disarm Iran-backed Hezbollah, according to Reuters.

The G7 discussions come as the UK imposed 70 new sanctions on Russia, including tankers transporting LNG, aiming to pressure Moscow's energy revenues, PunchNG reports. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer unveiled fresh sanctions on Russian shadow fleet vessels.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran ceasefire creates political space for Western allies to refocus on Russia sanctions. If the US rejoins the oil price cap enforcement regime, Russian export revenues face renewed pressure—but only if compliance mechanisms strengthen. The shadow fleet remains the key vulnerability.

sections.heading: Ukrainian Drone Strike Halts Operations at Moscow's Largest Refinery
sections.body:
A Ukrainian drone attack started a fire at the refinery that is the largest fuel supplier to the Moscow region on Tuesday, and two industry sources told Reuters that it had halted operations. Moscow's Mayor confirmed drone attacks across the city overnight.

The strike represents the latest in Ukraine's campaign to target Russian energy infrastructure. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said the drone attacks are 'coercion' for Russia to end the war, according to multiple outlets.

Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko, one of Putin's closest allies, said both sides in the Russia-Ukraine war must compromise, calling the conflict now in its fifth year with no end in sight, Reuters reports.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy infrastructure has become a central front in the Ukraine war. Refinery strikes directly impact Russian domestic fuel supply and military logistics, while also signaling that no energy asset is beyond Ukrainian reach. The Lukashenko comments hint at potential cracks in the Kremlin's information environment.

sections.heading: Russian Warship Fires Warning Shots at Yacht in English Channel
sections.body:
A Russian frigate fired warning shots at a UK-registered yacht in the English Channel on Tuesday, around 20 nautical miles south of the Isle of Wight, outside UK territorial waters, the BBC reports. The incident involved the Russian frigate Admiral Grigorovich, which fired from about 500 yards away.

British authorities received reports from the yacht but do not view the incident as linked to Sunday's Royal Marine interception of a Russian shadow fleet tanker carrying sanctioned oil in the Channel—the first operation of its kind by the British military.

The Russian warship has allegedly been operating in the Channel for a while, patrolling and escorting some of the Russian shadow fleet passing through the shipping lane. The HMS Mersey and HMS Tyne have been tracking the vessel.
sections.whyItMatters: The English Channel is a critical chokepoint for Russian shadow fleet vessels transiting to European ports. This incident—and the preceding Royal Marine interception—signal escalating maritime friction. Russian naval escorts for sanctioned tankers raise the risk of future confrontations with NATO naval forces.

sections.heading: Turkey Seeks Higher Oil Flows Through Iraq Pipeline
sections.body:
Turkey does not want an extension of the existing Kirkuk-Ceyhan oil pipeline agreement under current conditions, a senior Turkish official said, after Baghdad asked Ankara to extend it for at least a year, Reuters reports. Turkey is seeking higher oil flows through the crucial pipeline that runs through its territory from northern Iraq.

Separately, Turkey and Saudi Arabia signed agreements to advance the Hejaz Railway corridor project, reviving sections of the historic railway connecting Istanbul to Mecca, the New Arab reports. The rail corridor from Oman to Istanbul could provide an overland alternative to maritime routes—though analysts note it cannot replace Hormuz for bulk oil and gas transport.

Turkiye's energy import bill will rise by about $14 billion in 2026 due to higher oil and gas prices following the Gulf conflict disruption, according to regional media. Import dependence has locked the economy into exposure to external energy shocks.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is positioning itself as a critical energy transit corridor between the Gulf and Europe, leveraging its geography to maximize pipeline and rail revenue. The Kirkuk-Ceyhan leverage and the Hejaz Railway project both reflect Ankara's strategy to capture transit rents from multiple routes.

sections.heading: China Expands Export Controls Beyond Rare Earths
sections.body:
China is quietly expanding its export control regime against the United States and American allies, reaching beyond rare earth minerals to pinch choke points affecting key US industries, according to investors, business leaders and supply chain analysts cited by the Washington Post. The report notes stalled negotiations between SpaceX and a Chinese solar tech firm as one illustration.

The expansion comes as the American Battery Leadership Coalition launched to make sodium-ion batteries a US national priority, aiming to reduce dependence on Chinese-controlled supply chains, Yahoo Finance reports. Japan and Britain also agreed to deepen critical-mineral, technology, and defense cooperation amid concerns over China's export controls.

Lithium-rich areas in South America and Australia also contain substantial rare earth reserves in overlapping deposits, MSN reports, potentially easing some supply concerns—but development timelines remain long.
sections.whyItMatters: Beijing is signaling that rare earths were just the first layer of critical mineral leverage. By expanding controls to solar technology components and other materials, China raises the stakes for US industrial policy. Allied coordination on supply chains is accelerating, but years from delivering independence.

bottomLine: The US military's use of Iranian-style ship-to-ship transfers to move oil past Hormuz illustrates how chokepoint warfare forces even dominant naval powers into asymmetric adaptations. As the Iran ceasefire takes hold, the G7 is pivoting back to Russian oil sanctions pressure, while Ukraine demonstrates that Moscow's largest refinery remains vulnerable. The English Channel is becoming a friction point for Russian shadow fleet operations. Turkey continues leveraging its geography for energy transit revenue, and China is broadening its critical mineral export controls beyond rare earths. Energy geopolitics remain volatile across multiple fronts, with diplomatic breakthroughs not yet translating into physical market stability.
sources: Reuters, BBC, Politico Europe, Bloomberg, CNN, Washington Post, New Arab, Yahoo Finance, MSN, PunchNG, Financial Express, Tech Times, Crypto Briefing, UPI
